guest: Faraz Jaka, professional poker player (nickname: The Toilet)

business_ideas.idea: Poker House: rent property, run high-stakes poker games, teach other players

dynamics: Sam drives interview with probing questions about both poker success and personal development. Faraz tells detailed story arc from college dorm games through $10M career, with unusual depth on meditation, mental health, and personal discipline. The conversation balances technical poker strategy with introspection on emotional management and decision-making philosophy.
frameworks: Plus EV (Expected Value) thinking: probabilistic decision-making applied to dating, job offers, flight bookings, investments
Bankroll management: allocating portfolio percentage to any single bet (25-100 buy-ins for professionals)
On Tilt recognition and reset: identify emotional states and use physical tactics (wind sprints, breathing exercises) to reset
Process vs. Outcomes: separate decision quality from luck-influenced results; build confidence on decision analysis not short-term results
Sample size awareness: confidence in methods scales with data volume, not single wins
learnings: Obsessive competitive personality can be harnessed for excellence if disciplined; most people lack this drive naturally
Public humiliation and financial rock bottom create the necessary conditions for genuine learning
Peer groups learning from forums and WhatsApp study groups accelerate skill acquisition faster than solo study
Physical reset tactics (cold showers, wind sprints, breathing exercises) work better than logic for emotional recovery
Extended meditation training rewires decision-making under uncertainty and stress
Most decision failures in business are not about strategy but about emotional tilting and sunk cost fallacies
EV thinking universally applicable: career choices, relationships, and investments all reduce to probability-weighted outcomes

banger_quotes.quote: I felt instinctively that I was really good at this thing.
Don't bet on one company, have 25 bets per potential.
Most of your day your mind is just wandering wandering wandering.
You need to be sample size aware.

numbers_dropped: Built bankroll from $300 to $180K by age 19
Lost it all plus $50K debt at age 20
Six World Series Poker final tables
notable: Unusually prominent meditation arc; a 10-day silent Vipassana retreat in Thailand becomes central to his decision-making philosophy, not a side story. Explicit instruction on breathing techniques and physical reset tactics (wind sprints in tournament breaks) suggests self-optimization culture beyond poker, more in line with performance psychology than traditional poker narratives.
